agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform introducing a driver-facing earnings breakdown feature across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications, legal transparency obligations, and institutional approval requirements. This is not a single feature — it is three jurisdiction-specific implementations sharing a common UI concept.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation Visibility — a read-model over the existing payroll/settlement bounded context. This context does NOT own compensation calculation; it owns the presentation, itemization, and compliance-correct display of earnings data to drivers.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The guaranteed compensation component for a work period, independent of delivery volume. For employees (CA, DE), this is hourly/shift-based wage. For contractors (ID), this may be a per-order base rate." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation tied to each completed delivery. Distinct from base pay. In employee contexts, subject to overtime and minimum wage floor calculations." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "Gratuity paid by the customer and passed through to the driver. In California, tips may NOT be counted toward minimum wage obligations (Labor Code §351). Must always be displayed as a separate line item." },
      { "term": "Deduction", "definition": "Any amount subtracted from gross earnings. Legally distinct across jurisdictions: in California, every deduction requires written authorization (Labor Code §224). In Germany, deductions must conform to works council agreements. For Indonesian contractors, deductions are typically platform service fees governed by contract." },
      { "term": "Itemized Wage Statement", "definition": "California-specific legal artifact required under Labor Code §226. Must include gross wages, total hours, piece rates, deductions, net wages, pay period dates, and employer information. Failure to provide accurate statements triggers statutory penalties." },
      { "term": "Works Council Approval (Betriebsrat-Zustimmung)", "definition": "Mandatory co-determination right under German BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 (monitoring) and potentially No. 10/11 (pay principles). The works council must formally approve the feature BEFORE it is made available to any German driver. This is a hard deployment gate, not a courtesy review." },
      { "term": "Settlement Statement", "definition": "The contractor-equivalent of a wage statement in Indonesia. An account of earnings and platform fees for a period, governed by the service agreement rather than labor law." },
      { "term": "Earnings Period", "definition": "The time window over which earnings are aggregated for display. May differ by jurisdiction: pay period for employees, settlement cycle for contractors." },
      { "term": "Real-Time Earnings", "definition": "A running, up-to-date view of accumulated earnings within the current earnings period. Does NOT mean instant — it means 'current enough to reflect the last completed delivery before the driver checks.' Latency tolerance should be defined per jurisdiction." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "In California, the earnings breakdown MUST constitute a legally compliant itemized wage statement per Labor Code §226, including: gross wages earned, total hours worked, piece-rate units (deliveries), all deductions with descriptions, net wages, inclusive dates of the pay period, employee name and last four of SSN or employee ID, and employer legal name and address.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Failure to provide compliant wage statements exposes the platform to statutory penalties of $50 per employee for initial violation, $100 per employee per subsequent violation, plus attorney fees. This is a legal data-integrity requirement, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every earnings view rendered for a California driver includes all nine fields mandated by Labor Code §226(a)(1)-(9)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Tips MUST be displayed as a line item fully separated from base pay and bonuses. Tips must NEVER be included in or offset against base pay calculations.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California Labor Code §351 prohibits employers from using tips as a credit toward minimum wage. Commingling tips with base pay in display or calculation creates both legal liability and driver trust erosion. This is a physical constraint of the compensation model.",
      "testable_assertion": "The tip amount is always rendered as an independent line item and is excluded from any base pay or minimum wage floor computation."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, every deduction displayed MUST reference a prior written authorization on file. If no authorization exists, the deduction must not be applied and the display must flag an error state rather than showing an unauthorized deduction.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California Labor Code §224 makes unauthorized deductions from employee wages illegal. Displaying an unauthorized deduction implies it was applied, which is itself evidence of a violation.",
      "testable_assertion": "For each deduction line item shown to a California driver, the system can produce a corresponding signed authorization record."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The feature MUST NOT be deployed to any German driver until formal works council (Betriebsrat) approval is obtained and recorded in the system. This is a hard deployment gate.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "BetrVG §87 grants co-determination rights on monitoring systems and pay transparency tools. Deploying without approval is a violation of German labor law and can result in injunctions and nullification. The system would be in a legally corrupt state.",
      "testable_assertion": "The feature toggle for Germany requires a works_council_approval_date to be non-null and verified before any German driver can access the earnings breakdown view."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "In Germany, the earnings display format and content must conform to whatever the works council approves. The platform cannot unilaterally decide what to show — the approved specification becomes the contract.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The specific display format is a policy negotiated with the works council; it can change with a new agreement. But the requirement to HAVE an agreement is invariant (see BR-4).",
      "testable_assertion": "The German earnings display template is configurable and loaded from a jurisdiction-specific configuration that maps to the works council agreement version."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "In Indonesia, the earnings breakdown is a business feature, not a legal obligation. The display should show base rate, per-delivery earnings, tips, and platform service fees (not 'deductions' — contractors have fees, not deductions).",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a voluntary transparency feature. The platform could change the level of detail shown to Indonesian contractors as a business decision without legal consequence.",
      "testable_assertion": "Indonesian driver earnings views label platform charges as 'Service Fees' not 'Deductions', and the feature can be independently enabled or disabled for the Indonesia market."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The domain language for subtracted amounts MUST differ by driver classification: 'Deductions' for employees (CA, DE), 'Service Fees' or 'Platform Fees' for contractors (ID). Using employee-oriented language for contractors risks implying an employment relationship.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Misclassification litigation is the highest-value legal risk in gig economy platforms. Using the word 'deduction' for a contractor implies employer-employee wage treatment and can be used as evidence of misclassification. This is a data integrity issue — the wrong label corrupts the legal meaning.",
      "testable_assertion": "The UI label for subtracted amounts is resolved from the driver's jurisdiction and classification, and the term 'Deduction' never appears for a driver classified as an independent contractor."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "California drivers who are employees must see a minimum wage guarantee line if their base pay plus per-delivery bonuses for the period fall below the applicable minimum wage floor (state or local, whichever is higher).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Under AB5, drivers are employees entitled to minimum wage. If the platform pays piece rates, it must guarantee the floor and show the adjustment transparently. Hiding this violates the transparency mandate.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a California driver's calculated hourly earnings (base + bonuses / hours worked) fall below the applicable minimum wage, a 'Minimum Wage Adjustment' line item appears bringing total to at least the minimum wage."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "Earnings data displayed must reflect completed, confirmed deliveries only. Pending, disputed, or cancelled deliveries must not be included in the earnings total but may be shown separately in a 'Pending' section.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying unconfirmed earnings as actual earnings is financially misleading and creates reconciliation disputes. The system's earnings state would be inaccurate.",
      "testable_assertion": "The earnings total only includes deliveries with status = 'completed' and 'confirmed'. Deliveries in 'pending', 'disputed', or 'cancelled' status are excluded from totals."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver in California with 5 completed deliveries in the current pay period",
      "when": "The driver opens the earnings breakdown view",
      "then": "The view displays all nine Labor Code §226 required fields: gross wages, hours worked, piece-rate count (5), itemized deductions with descriptions, net wages, pay period dates, driver name/ID, and employer legal entity name and address"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver in California who received $15 in tips across deliveries",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is rendered",
      "then": "Tips appear as a standalone line item ($15.00), are NOT added to base pay, and are NOT factored into the minimum wage floor calculation"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The Germany market where works council approval has NOT been granted",
      "when": "A German driver attempts to access the earnings breakdown feature",
      "then": "The feature is not visible or accessible; the system returns the existing compensation view (or no view) rather than the new breakdown"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "The Germany market where works council approval HAS been granted with a specific display specification",
      "when": "A German driver opens the earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The display conforms exactly to the works-council-approved template, not the generic platform template"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver in Indonesia classified as an independent contractor with a 15% platform service fee",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The subtracted amount is labeled 'Service Fee' (never 'Deduction'), and no employer-specific language (wages, hours worked, overtime) appears anywhere in the view"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A California driver whose hourly earnings for the pay period calculate to $14.50/hr, below the $16.00 state minimum wage",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is rendered",
      "then": "A 'Minimum Wage Adjustment' line item appears showing the top-up amount, and the total reflects at least $16.00/hr equivalent"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A driver with 3 completed deliveries and 1 delivery in 'disputed' status",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is rendered",
      "then": "The earnings total reflects only the 3 completed deliveries; the disputed delivery appears separately and is not included in the total"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A California driver with a payroll deduction for equipment rental",
      "when": "The deduction is displayed in the earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The deduction line item links to or references the signed written authorization, and if no authorization exists, the system raises an error rather than displaying the deduction"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A driver relocates from Indonesia to California mid-contract", "expected_behavior": "The system must reclassify the driver and switch the entire earnings display to the California employee template, including retroactive compliance for any California-based work. The old contractor view must no longer be accessible for California earnings periods." },
    { "scenario": "Works council approval in Germany is revoked or expires", "expected_behavior": "The feature must be immediately disabled for all German drivers. The system should treat revocation the same as never having approval — hard gate closed." },
    { "scenario": "A California driver works in a city with a higher local minimum wage than the state minimum", "expected_behavior": "The minimum wage adjustment must use the higher local rate (e.g., San Francisco $18.67 vs state $16.00). The system must resolve the applicable rate by work location, not driver home address." },
    { "scenario": "A tip is adjusted or reversed after the driver has already seen it in the real-time view", "expected_behavior": "The adjustment must appear as a separate line item (e.g., 'Tip Adjustment -$3.00') in the next refresh, never silently reduce the previously displayed total without explanation." },
    { "scenario": "Platform service fee percentage changes mid-settlement-cycle for Indonesian contractors", "expected_behavior": "Deliveries completed before the change retain the old fee rate; deliveries after use the new rate. The breakdown must show both rates if applicable within the same period." },
    { "scenario": "A California driver works zero deliveries but is on-shift for 4 hours", "expected_behavior": "Base pay for hours worked must still appear (reporting time pay). The breakdown shows hours, base pay at minimum wage or contracted rate, zero bonuses, zero tips, and any applicable deductions." },
    { "scenario": "Real-time view is accessed during active payroll processing", "expected_behavior": "The view must show a clear 'Processing' state for the affected period rather than showing partial or stale data. Completed prior periods remain viewable." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "HIGH. California non-compliance with §226 carries penalties of $50-$100 per driver per pay period plus attorney fees in class actions. For a fleet of 10,000 CA drivers, a single missed field could generate $500K-$1M in statutory penalties per pay period. Correct implementation protects against this. Conversely, transparency builds driver retention, reducing the $3,000-$5,000 per-driver recruitment cost.",
    "operational": "SIGNIFICANT. This is effectively three distinct feature rollouts with different timelines. Germany cannot launch until works council process completes (weeks to months). California must launch with full legal compliance from day one — no 'MVP' that omits required fields. Indonesia can launch independently. Operations teams need jurisdiction-specific rollout plans, not a single global launch.",
    "user": "Driver trust and retention are directly tied to earnings transparency. Drivers who understand their pay are less likely to churn and more likely to accept shifts. However, showing deductions or fees prominently may increase driver complaints initially — support teams need preparation. Incorrect or misleading displays will destroy trust faster than no display at all.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL legal risk in California (wage statement violations, misclassification evidence). CRITICAL process risk in Germany (works council injunction). MODERATE misclassification risk in Indonesia if employee-oriented language leaks into the contractor UI. MODERATE data accuracy risk if the real-time view shows unconfirmed earnings that are later adjusted.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Gain legally mandated transparency they are entitled to. May surface existing payroll discrepancies that require remediation." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Delayed access until works council approves. Must not be shown any version of the feature prematurely." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Gain voluntary transparency. Language must reinforce contractor relationship, not undermine it." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Must validate California template against §226 requirements. Must manage works council negotiation timeline in Germany. Must review Indonesian UI for misclassification risk." },
      { "group": "Works Council (Germany)", "impact": "Has co-determination authority. Must be engaged early. Their feedback may fundamentally alter the German version of the feature." },
      { "group": "Payroll/Finance", "impact": "Must ensure the data feeding the display is accurate to the penny. Any discrepancy between displayed earnings and actual deposits will generate disputes." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will see increased driver inquiries when deductions/fees become visible. Needs training materials and escalation paths for disputes." },
      { "group": "Product Team", "impact": "Must accept that this is not one feature but three jurisdiction-specific implementations with a shared design system. A single global spec will fail." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec is dangerously underspecified for a feature with this level of legal exposure. It treats three fundamentally different legal regimes as one feature. Critical details are missing: what specific deductions exist, what the works council timeline is, what 'real time' means in latency terms, and whether legal counsel has reviewed the California requirements. The spec does not mention Labor Code §226 at all, which is the single most important compliance requirement. I am confident in the domain rules I have identified, but I have low confidence that the spec as written will lead to a compliant implementation without significant elaboration.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "California wage statement requirements under §226 are well-established law",
      "Works council approval is a hard prerequisite in Germany — this is unambiguous",
      "Tip separation from base pay is a clear legal requirement in California",
      "Contractor vs employee language distinction is a well-understood misclassification risk"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What specific deductions the platform applies and whether authorizations exist",
      "Whether the platform's current payroll system can provide data at the granularity needed for §226 compliance",
      "The works council's likely requirements — these cannot be predicted and may change the German feature entirely",
      "What 'real time' means in practice — sub-second? per-delivery? per-hour? This has major architecture implications",
      "Whether the platform has already obtained legal review of California requirements",
      "Local minimum wage resolution — does the platform track delivery locations at city-level granularity?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform already has a payroll/settlement system that calculates these earnings components — this feature is a read-model, not a new calculation engine",
      "Driver classification (employee vs contractor) is already determined and stored per driver per jurisdiction",
      "The platform has legal counsel who will review the California implementation before launch",
      "The works council engagement in Germany has not yet begun, making the Germany timeline the longest"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define 'real time.' For California employees, earnings may need to reconcile with payroll cycles (bi-weekly). For Indonesian contractors, settlement may be daily. The architecture and UX differ radically depending on whether 'real time' means per-delivery push updates, periodic polling, or simply 'current pay period to date refreshed on page load.'",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product Team", "Engineering", "Drivers"],
      "options": ["Per-delivery update (most complex, highest driver satisfaction)", "Per-hour batch refresh (moderate complexity)", "On-demand refresh of current period totals (simplest)"],
      "recommendation": "Define 'real time' as 'current earnings period, updated within 5 minutes of delivery completion, refreshed on page load.' This balances driver expectations with system feasibility."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'deductions' without specifying what types of deductions exist on this platform. The entire California compliance model depends on knowing what is being deducted and whether written authorizations exist. Equipment fees? Insurance? Tax withholding? Advances? Each has different legal treatment.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Payroll/Finance", "Drivers (California)"],
      "options": ["Enumerate all current deduction types and verify authorization status before building", "Build generic deduction display and populate later"],
      "recommendation": "MUST enumerate all deduction types first. Building a generic display without knowing the deduction landscape risks displaying unauthorized deductions, which is a Labor Code §224 violation."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of whether legal counsel has reviewed California Labor Code §226 requirements for this feature. The spec describes a 'breakdown' but §226 requires nine specific fields. Without legal sign-off, we risk building a feature that is useful but non-compliant.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Product Team", "Drivers (California)"],
      "options": ["Proceed with engineering interpretation of §226", "Block California development until legal review is complete"],
      "recommendation": "Block California development until legal has reviewed and signed off on the specific fields, format, and delivery mechanism. The penalty exposure is too high for engineering to interpret labor law."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The status of works council engagement in Germany is unknown. If not yet initiated, this is a multi-month blocker. If already underway, their requirements may have already constrained the German design. The feature cannot be designed for Germany without this input.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Works Council (Germany)", "Legal/Compliance", "Product Team", "Drivers (Germany)"],
      "options": ["Design Germany version speculatively and adapt after works council feedback", "Defer Germany entirely until works council process concludes", "Begin works council engagement now with a draft proposal"],
      "recommendation": "Begin works council engagement immediately with a draft proposal. Design the German feature as a configurable template that can be adjusted based on their feedback without re-engineering."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
